steps.content:
| Property | Value | CYP2C9 Inhibitor Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| MW | 155.25 Da | 200–600 Da |
| LogP | 1.13 | 2–5 (moderate lipophilicity) |
| TPSA | 27.96 Å² | 40–120 Å² |
| HBD | 0 | 0–3 |
| HBA | 3 | 2–7 |
| Aromatic rings | **0** | **1–3 (critical)** |
| Fsp3 | 0.88 | Typically < 0.5 |

**Key functional groups identified:**
1. **Carbodiimide (N=C=N)** — a cumulated double bond system, highly reactive, not a typical pharmacophore for CYP binding
2. **Tertiary amine (–N(CH₃)₂)** — basic group, pKa ~9; will be **protonated/cationic** at physiological pH
3. **Ethylamine (–NHCH₂CH₃)** — another basic nitrogen
4. **Propyl chain** — flexible aliphatic linker, no rigidity
**Why this molecule should NOT inhibit CYP2C9:**

1. **No aromatic system.** CYP2C9 has a hydrophobic, planar active site pocket that accommodates flat aromatic substrates/inhibitors (warfarin, fluconazole, sulfaphenazole). This molecule is entirely aliphatic with Fsp3 = 0.88 — it cannot make the critical π-stacking or hydrophobic interactions with the active-site residues (Phe100, Phe114, Phe476).

2. **Basic/cationic character.** CYP2C9 preferentially binds **acidic or neutral** molecules (the canonical substrates are weak acids like warfarin, phenytoin, NSAIDs). The dimethylamino group (pKa ~9) ensures this molecule is **positively charged** at pH 7.4, creating electrostatic repulsion with the positively charged active-site residues.

3. **Too small and too flexible.** At 155 Da, this molecule is well below the typical MW range for CYP2C9 inhibitors. The high rotational freedom (5 rotatable bonds for only 11 heavy atoms) means poor shape complementarity with the CYP2C9 binding pocket.
4. **No known structural alerts.** None of the CYP2C9 alerts (sulfonamides, coumarins, oxicams, aromatic amides) are present.
The ADMET ML models strongly confirm the structural reasoning:

| CYP Isoform | Inhibition Probability |
|---|---|
| **CYP2C9** | **0.00** |
| CYP1A2 | 0.00 |
| CYP2C19 | 0.01 |
| CYP3A4 | 0.00 |
| CYP2D6 | 0.50 (borderline — consistent with basic amine character) |

The CYP2C9 probability of **0.00** is as definitive as the model can be. Note CYP2D6 shows 0.50, which makes pharmacological sense — CYP2D6 *does* preferentially metabolize basic amines, so some interaction there is plausible. This internal consistency increases confidence in the predictions.
**PREDICTION: negative** — this molecule is NOT an inhibitor of CYP2C9.
**CONFIDENCE: high** — structural analysis and computational prediction are in complete agreement; every relevant feature argues against CYP2C9 inhibition.

**KEY_FACTORS:**
1. **Complete absence of aromatic rings** — CYP2C9 requires planar aromatic systems for active-site binding; Fsp3 = 0.88 means this is an overwhelmingly aliphatic molecule
2. **Basic/cationic character** — the tertiary dimethylamine (pKa ~9) ensures the molecule is protonated at physiological pH, electrostatically disfavoring the CYP2C9 active site which prefers acidic/neutral substrates
3. **Low molecular weight (155 Da) and high flexibility** — insufficient size and shape complementarity to occupy the CYP2C9 binding pocket effectively
